LOtYTPOC
March 8, L99 0
General Counsel
Fair PoIitical Practices Commission
428 South J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814
Re: Request for Advice Relating to Potential Conflict of Interests
Dear General Counsel :
In order to assist two members of the City Council of the City of
Lompoc, our office has concluded that it would be desireable to
request written advice from your office 
in accordance with
Government Code S8311-4, regarding conflict of interest guestions.
Both Councilmembers, J.D. Snith and Christa Marks, have expressly
authorized this request for an advice letter. 
For your assj,stance,
I will set forth the facts and circumstances giving rise to this
request. 
I am also attaching relevant documents for your
reference .
Introducti.on and overvie$r.
A recent development proposal in the City's mediurn density, R-2
zone, is creating a certain amount of controversy. Brieflt, 
the
deveLoperts proposal (hereinafter referred to for convenience as
rrthe Projecttr) involves taking a Lo,5oo sqluare foot 1ot that
currently has two existing units on it, adding a third unit, and
subdividing the units for oqrnership purposes. The procedures that
he is using includes application of what we call our trpDr zone as
an overlay over the R-2, medium density designation. Without going
into details unnecessary for your evaluation, simply put the rpDi
zoning concept alLows flexibility 
in rneeting design standards,
which is necessary in order for the applicant to do his project.
(Reference Sections 77OO et seq. of the Lompoc City code, copies
of which are attached.) 
Although these zoning and land use
development procedures have existed in the Cityts code for many
years, my understanding is that the project represents a new way
of using these developrTrent tools in the City of Lonpoc.
The Project has been vigorously opposed by a large group of
citizens. 
fn addition to criticizing 
the specific design, as j-t
relates to the distance between the proposed units and other
CITY HALL, 1OO CIVIC CENTER PLAZA, P.O. BOX 8001, LOMPOC, CA 93438.8001
(805) 736-1261; FAX: (805) 735-5347
VALLEY OF FLOWERS


March 8, L99 0
Paqe 2
features, the opponents have expressed considerable concern
regarding the rrprecedencert that witl be set if the Project is
allowed to proceed. They have asserted that others with 1,0,500
square foot lots with only two units, could do the same thing.
Others have expressed concern about the wisdom of using the trPDtr
on sma11 lots in existing neighborhoods. Accordingly, on January
2, l-990 the Lompoc City Council adopted a moratorium on
developments in the R-2 zone and the use of the nPDtr concept. The
Project was referred back to Staff and the Planning Comnission for
their review. 
Since that tirne, the moratorium has also been
extended to the R-3, high-density residential zone,
Both the Project and a series of possible amendments to the Zoning
Ordinance are now working their way back to the City Council- for
their consideration. (See attached report to Planning Commission,
which details the proposed anendments to the Zoning Ordinance. )
It has now been brought to light that two City Councilmembers own
property zoned R-2 and R-3. The question has arisen as to whether
they can participate when the City Council- considers both the
Project and the proposed changes to the Zoning Ordinance.
Accordingly, written advice from your office is being sought to
clarify the question of those councilmernbersr participation.
Propertv Owned by Council,member llarks.
Councilmernber Marks olrns t$ro properties that may be affected.
First, she is the owner of a site that is L7,500 square feet that
is zoned R-3. The 1ot consists of her residence and two additional
rental units. 
Under the density standards for the R-3 zone in the
existing Code, Planning Departrnent staff has indicated she could
developnent a maximum of eight dwelling units on the site. 
Her
residence also has been designated a loca1 historical 
landmark,
which results in certain restrictions being irnposed on her house.
These restrictions 
do not extend to the rest of the property
(reference attached Resolution 3495(85). The attached nap of the
City may be of assistance in putting the location of her residence
in perspective (the pencil drawn circle is a 2500 sq. ft. radius
around her property) .
council-member ltlarks also owns a four-unit rental property that is
zoned R-2. I am informed by the Ptanning Department staff that the
lot area consists of 7,000 square feet. The current standards for
the R-2 zone allow one dwell-ing unit for each 3,000 square feet of
lot area. Based upon this standard, only two dweLling units would
be allowed to be developed on the site. 
Accordingly, the property
has a rrlegal , non-conformingrr status. Staff has further advised
that under the Zoning ordinance provisions rel-ating to non-
confoming uses and structures, they nay be continued but not


Uarch 8, 1990
Page 3
expanded. The Planning Department staff has also advised that in
their vieo, properties that contain non-conforming uses and/or
structures voufd not be eLigible for deveLopnent using the 'rPD'l
zoning designation (see attached nenorandum fron Acting Associate
Planner Arleen Pelster). 
No changes to the Zoning Ordinance,
relating to legal, non-confoming uses and structures, are being
proposed at this time.
Both of the properties or4,ned by Councilrnernber Marks are located
nore than 2500 feet fron the Project. In addition, lrhile a range
of possible zoning ordinance Arnendments have been prepared for
Ptanning cornrnission and city councit consideration, the actual
densities, as far as the nurnber of unj-ts that can be developed on
a site, are not being proposed to be increased or decreased.
Depending on uhat other changes are nade to the code, the
development potential of the R-3 site could be affected. As to the
lega1, non-conforning R-2 property, the PLanning Departrnent has
advised that it does not appear that any of the changes being
proposed would change its development potential.
Propertv Olrned bv Councilnenber Smith.
councilrnenber smith ouns a lot that is zoned R-2 that consists of
7,000 square feet. 
According to councilnenber smith, there are
three existing units on the site. Based upon the density standards
for the R-2 zone, this property could ohly be developed i,ith tlro
units, therefore it is also Ilegal, non-conforning. I 
Thus, the
analysis presented for the IegaI, non-conforming property oL,ned by
Councilmember llarks, also appl-ies to Councilnenber Snj.th ' s
property.
councilnenber Snithrs property is also located more than 2500 feet
from the proposed developnent project.
ABplication of Requlations to Possible Conflicts.
I realize the difficulty in analyzing the foregoing in retation to
the FPPC Regulations. Certainly, one problem wiII be that your
offj.ce is generally unfarniliar with the City,s Zoning Ordina;ce.
Accordingly, the following observations are offered for your
assistance.
We recognize that there are tuo separate natters that need to be
analyzed. The first is the abili.ty of the Councilrnenbers to
participate on a decision for the project. 
The second is the
question of changes to the Zoning Ordi;ance that apply generally
to all properties situated in the city that are zoned R-2 and R-3:


March 8, 1990
Paqe 4
Section l-8702.3 -- ltaterialitv 
and Property Indj-rectly Involved in
the Decision.
As noted, all- three of the properties in question are located more
than 2500 feet fron the Project. Therefore, it would appear that
Section 18702.3(b), of the Regulations of the Fair Political
Practices Commission, is controlling in determining whether the
effect of a decision on the Project will be material .
As to the properties owned by Councilmernbers uarks and Smith that
are zoned 1ega1 , non-conforming, the Planning Department staff has
concluded that their status precludes developments as a trPDr
project. 
Therefore, it would not appear that a decision could
affect the market value of the property in the amount set forth in
Section la702.3 (a) (3) (A) or (a) (3) (B) , or other$rise indirectly
effect the property.
I find a decision on the Project and its potentj.al effect on
Councihnember Markst R-3 property, that has the potential for as
many as eight units, more difficult 
to analyze. At first 
b1ush,
aIl the talk about the riprecedencerr of the decision would seen to
suggest that action on the Project rnay indeed inpact the value of
this piece of property, It should be noted, holrever, that this
property is already zoned at a higher density, and is large enough
to allow for additional development, regardless of what happens to
the Project. 
Moreover, aLthough there is a lot of talk of
tiprecedence, tr the fact is that the opportunity to use the existing
city Codes to develop the property has always been there. The xPDx
zone is site specific, and considered on a case by case basis,
councilmember Marks could always have applied to develop the
property under the existing regulations. In any event, I assume
that the answer wiLl be a technical one that has to be answered by
an appraiser or real estate professional . 
The question, as I
understand it, 
is whether the value of the property !,/i11 be
increa sed
18702.3 (a)
Proj ect.
or
(3)In
decreased in the amounts set forth in Sections
(A) or (a) (3) (B), as a result of action on the
any event, I have advised Councilrnenber Marks to
discuss her property, and possible financial effect of any decision
on the Project, with real estate professionals and other that rnight
be knowledgeable in that regard.
AIso, under Section 18702.3(b), in addition to the threshold
financial effect, either the effect must not be substantially the
sane as the effect of at least 25* of properties within a 25OO foot
radius of the property in question, or there are not at least ten
properties under separate ownership within 25OO feet. 
I 
am
inforrued by the planning Department itaff that they are unable to
provide any analysis regarding this second elenent. Arthough there


March 8, 1990
Page 5
are in all likelihood more than ten properties hrithin 2500 feet
under separate ownership (we have determined that there are 410
parcels in that area), the Planning Department staff has indicated
that there are practical problems with determining the development
potential of property located within 2500 feet. In all candor, in
their view it is an unworkable formula.
section 18702.1 -- litaterial Financial Effect on Propertv Directlv
Involved.
As to the effect of the proposed changes to the zoning ordinance,
it would appear that section 18702.1 is controlling. 
In discussing
this with one of your staff attorneys, however, it was indicated
that section L8702.3 relating to property indirectty invol-ved in
a decision might also come into p1ay. Frankly, I am somewhat
confused by this. 
I also, however, understand that these
provj,sions are relatively new, and have not undergone extensive
application to factual situations.
The code changes that are being discussed, j,n my view, directly
involve the properties in question. This is because they are the
rul-es that will govern the manner in which these properties may be
devel,oped and used. 
section La102.1(a) (3) (E) appears to
specifically exempt amendments to zoning ordinances or other land
use regulations that are applicable to all other properties
designated in a particular zoning category, fron zoning and
rezoning actions that uright effect property directly involved in
a decision. 
That woutd appear to be the case in the instant
matter, since what is conternplated are changes to the rules and
regulations that would apply to all R-2 and R-3 properties in the
city.
I am unclear how Section )-A702.3(c) night also app1y. That section
provides that s/here the official rs property is indirecttv involved,
and the decision does not involve a subject property frorn which
distances can be determined, the threshold rnonetary standards
contained in Subdivision (a) (3) apply. If this is the case, it is
not inconceivable that a specific decision on the rules and
regulations for development proj ects in the R-2 and R-3 would have
the required monetary inpact on Councilmember Markst R-3 property,
This appears to lead to the question of the role of Section l-8703,
relating to effect on the public generally, and how it enters into
the analysis.
If I understand things correctly, 
even
financial 
effect, 
disgual ification 
is if there is a material
not required if 
the


l.Iarch 8, 1990
Page 6
officialrs interest is effected in a substantially sirnilar manner
as a significant segment of the public. I an unclear as to how
this rule i6 to be applied.
For your assistance, the folLoi^'ing facts, according to the Plahning
Departnent, are provided: 
The city has a population of
approximately 33,600. Its area covers 10.94 square rniles, and it
contains approxirnately 7004 acres. It is divided into several
conunercial, industrial and residential zohes. There are four
residential zones (LA, R-1, R-2 and R-3). There are approxihately
1.99.66 acres of R-2 property and 204.34 acres of R-3. I atn
inforrned by the Planning Department that based upon a study
conducted in January, l-989, there are 492 parcels of Land in the
city zoned R-2 and 608 zoned R-3. This study also deterhined that
208 R-2 lots and 254 R-3 lots had rtdevelopnent potential.I 
This
lias based upon the Planning Departu[ent's analysis that additional
units could be added to a parcel, based upon what has been built,
as compared to the density allowed by the Zoninq Ordinance.
A decision on the Project arquably rri1l effect all of those parcels
that have rrdevefopnent potentiall in a similar manner. Yet, each
piece of property vi1l have its or^rn characteristics (i.e., size,
shape, condition and nature of existing structures, etc.) that
night skev this conclusion. The dilerula appears to be how far to
go in analyzing the question of trsinilartr effect when dealing wi-th
a land use/zoning question in relation to property that is
sornething other than ralr land. On the surface, however, every
property in the City in the sane zoning category vould appear to
be inpacted in a siloilar rnanner.
It lras also suggested to ne that a no vote on the project, or
disal"lorring develophent sinilar to the Project, could have the
effect of increasinq the value of existing units (i.e. if
opportunities to develop additional housing is restricted, existing
housing is nore valuable). lIy sense is that the ansrrer to thia
point also lies in Section 1.8703. Everyone olrning R-2 and R-3
units (and indeed R-1, single family houses) are effacted the same
vay.
Fina11y, the foregoing considerations also seem to apply to changes
to the Zoning Ordinance that are appticable to all pioperty in tne
city in the same zoning cateqory. In fact, as you-will note fron
the attached report detailing the staff recommen-ded changes to the
Code, the proposed fDevelopment Reviewr process vou]d apply to
virtually att properties it the city (the only exception lli-"g u
single family home on an individual fbt). wnat-is meant by section
18703 has. significant implications beyond the instant [uestion,
slnce cities are frequently involved j.n changes to ceneral plans


Uarch 8, L99o
Page 7
and Zoning Ordj.nances that apply to the City as a whole (as opposed
to rezoning a particutar parcel of property). If certain actions
on specific provisions to the Zoning ordinance uright be construed
as a conflict, 
how can that be handled in any practical way?
Accordingrly, I hope that your officers approach to this issue
refl,ects a recognition of the need for practical, workable formulas
and ru1es.
I hope the foregoing thoughts will be of assistance. On behalf of
Councilmembers llarks and Snith, thank you for your offj,ce's
assistance with regard to providing guidance so that improper
conduct is avoided. 
If 
either ny office, 
or the Planning
Department, can provide you with any additional clarification
regarding the foregoing, please feel free to contact me at
(805) 736-1261, ext. 23!.
{ery truly yours,
l)"aNA=-
city Attorney

Councilnenber Srnith
